source.title: The integration of negative affect, pain and cognitive control in the cingulate cortex

confidence_rationale: Meta-analysis of 380+ neuroimaging studies demonstrating dACC/aMCC as the common substrate for pain, negative affect, AND cognitive control -- strongest possible evidence that the cingulate serves as a genuine integration hub rather than a region-of-responsibility for one of these three. Very high source quality (large-scale quantitative meta-analysis). Direct mapping: dACC/aMCC is the z_harm_a action-selection consumer substrate.
failure_signatures: A substrate that keeps affective-pain processing, action-conflict monitoring, and cognitive-control allocation in separate modules will fail to reproduce the Shackman meta-analytic convergence -- it will also fail to scale: the biological economy of integrating these three signals in one region exists because they are computationally interdependent.

The 'adaptive control hypothesis' Shackman proposes says dACC drives behavioural adjustment scaled to negative affect. A consumer of z_harm_a that does not *also* have access to goal/reward signals and cognitive-conflict signals will misfire: it will adjust behaviour whenever affective pain is high, even when no action is available to reduce it -- the opposite of what adaptive control demands.

mapping.source_claim_statement: Quantitative meta-analysis of 380+ fMRI/PET studies across three domains (pain, negative affect, cognitive control) demonstrates a common cingulate substrate: dACC/aMCC activates for all three with overlapping peak coordinates. The authors propose the 'adaptive control hypothesis': dACC integrates negative affect (including pain) with cognitive-control demands to generate behavioural adjustments whose magnitude and direction track the current need for control. dACC is the hub where 'something is aversive' meets 'what should we do differently.'
mapping.ree_translation: This is the paper that specifies what the z_harm_a *action-selection* consumer should look like. It is not a pure cost adder (Option A alone), because that does not integrate cognitive control. It is not a pure forward-rollout cost (Option B alone), because that does not integrate conflict. It is the joint computation: affective cost + conflict + control demand -> behavioural adjustment magnitude. ree-v3 has none of this integration. Trajectory scoring in E3 adds harm cost but does not read cognitive conflict or control demand. The new SD cluster must specify a dACC-analog module that reads (z_harm_a, z_conflict, z_control_demand) and writes an adjustment signal that reshapes trajectory selection at the policy level.
mapping.mapping_caveat: Shackman's meta-analysis establishes co-localization but not the computational form of the integration. The 'adaptive control hypothesis' is a framework, not a specific algorithm. Mapping to ree-v3 requires committing to a computational form -- probably an expected-value-of-control type calculation (Shenhav 2013) -- that the meta-analysis itself does not pin down. Also, the meta-analysis is over human fMRI, which has limited temporal resolution; the specific dynamics of integration (which signal enters first, how they combine) require electrophysiology.
mapping.source_context: Coordinate-based quantitative meta-analysis (ALE / activation likelihood estimation) over 380+ neuroimaging studies, human subjects.

confidence_components.notes: Meta-analysis is the highest-confidence form of integrative evidence; this is the single strongest argument for treating the cingulate as an integration substrate. Mapping is strong in principle but requires additional computational specification (from Shenhav/Rushworth lines of work) to become implementable.
